original_hypothesis.title: LiquidityGauge constructor may be griefed/DoSed by malicious underlying token symbol() behavior, breaking gauge deployment from LendingLedger
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: LiquidityGauge constructor calls `ERC20(_underlyingToken).symbol()` twice to build name/symbol. If the underlying token's symbol() is non-standard (reverts, returns extremely large data, or consumes excessive gas), gauge deployment via LendingLedger.whiteListLendingMarket(..., _hasGauge=true) can revert, preventing whitelisting setup.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a deployment-time and configuration-time integration hazard. While standard ERC20 tokens typically have safe symbol(), real-world tokens sometimes revert or are proxy-upgradeable; this can block market onboarding or emergency redeployments.
deployment_assumptions: Governance attempts to whitelist a market with _hasGauge=true
Underlying token at _market has a non-standard or adversarial symbol() implementation
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: LiquidityGauge constructor builds strings via external calls: `string.concat(ERC20(_underlyingToken).symbol(), ...)` twice; no try/catch or fallback behavior.

proof_of_signal.pattern: untrusted external call in constructor used for metadata assembly without safeguards

preconditions: Underlying token contract address is controlled by a malicious party or is non-standard
Governance calls whitelist with _hasGauge=true (deploys new LiquidityGauge)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external call to token metadata in constructor
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no cached/parameterized name & symbol; no try/catch fallback

analysis_questions: Are whitelisted markets guaranteed to have standard symbol()? (doc says standard ERC20, but not enforced)
Should the gauge name/symbol be provided directly by governance to avoid external calls?
validation_requirements: Test with an ERC20 that reverts on symbol() and attempt whitelist with _hasGauge=true
Test with symbol() returning very large string to estimate gas and revert risks

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys a lending market token with malicious symbol() and convinces governance to whitelist it; gauge deployment reverts repeatedly, preventing onboarding and potentially blocking governance workflows.
prerequisites: Governance chooses a problematic underlying token/market

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Operational: prevents market gauge deployment; may delay emissions and integrations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users of that market; governance operations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Whitelist without gauge or deploy a patched gauge implementation

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for non-standard ERC20 metadata methods during gauge deployment

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: constructor gas with large symbol strings
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observe reverts when whitelisting markets with gauges

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
